system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
# Task C - Health app data privacy policy

## Brief

A direct-to-consumer health app (~2M monthly active users) is launching an AI-powered health coaching feature. The product team must choose a primary data policy that governs how user health data is processed by the AI. The choice has implications for capability, privacy, regulatory exposure, cost, and trust. The CEO asks for a primary recommendation.

## Options (elements E1-E5, anonymized later)

**Option A:** Local-only processing. All AI inference happens on the user's device. No health data leaves the phone. The model is smaller (capability ceiling 60-70% of cloud-equivalent) but privacy is structurally maximal.

**Option B:** Encrypted cloud with no training. User health data sent to the cloud encrypted, decrypted only at inference time in a confidential-computing enclave, never used to train any model. Full capability, ~3x infrastructure cost.

**Option C:** Cloud with opt-out training. Data sent to the cloud, used for personalization by default, used for model improvement unless user opts out. Full capability, standard cost, regulatory grey area in some jurisdictions.

**Option D:** Cloud with opt-in training only. Data sent to the cloud and used for personalization; used for model improvement only with explicit opt-in. Default-on opt-in flow with clear UX. Capability slightly degraded vs C by default (less training data).

**Option E:** Federated learning. Personalization happens locally; aggregate model updates sent to cloud, individual data does not leave the device. Privacy preserved at architectural level. Capability and reliability still under active research; deployment complexity is high.

## Task for each agent

Recommend the most appropriate policy and explain why. 200-400 words. Advocate a position rather than enumerating all options.

## Why this task

- Different domain from A (capital allocation) and B (organizational diagnosis): product ethics with regulatory and technical dimensions.
- Multi-axis tradeoffs: privacy / capability / cost / trust / complexity.
- No clear right answer; reasonable agents will weight axes differently.
- 5 elements suitable for triadic procedure.
- Cross-validates whether method works on a third domain not seen in pilot.


Provide your recommendation in 200-400 words. Advocate for a specific position; do not enumerate all options.
raw_response_content:
# Recommendation: Option D (Cloud with opt-in training only)

For a consumer health app at 2M MAU launching an AI coaching feature, **Option D is the right primary policy**. It is the only option that simultaneously preserves full product capability, keeps cost manageable, builds defensible trust, and stays clearly on the right side of regulators in every major jurisdiction.

The decisive consideration is that health coaching quality matters morally, not just commercially. A locally-constrained model (A) capped at 60-70% of cloud capability means worse coaching for 2M people whose health outcomes depend on it. Federated learning (E) is intellectually appealing but research-stage; betting a launch on it invites reliability problems and shifting privacy guarantees as the technique matures. Both options sacrifice too much capability for privacy gains that Option D can largely match through policy and architecture.

Among cloud options, C is disqualified: opt-out training on health data is a regulatory and reputational time bomb. GDPR, the evolving US state health privacy laws (WA My Health My Data, etc.), and HIPAA-adjacent expectations all push toward explicit consent for secondary uses. One enforcement action or investigative article ("app trains AI on your depression logs by default") ends the company.

Option B is the principled maximalist choice and deserves serious consideration, but 3x infrastructure cost at 2M MAU is a material drag, and "never train on any data" forecloses the personalization-via-learning loop that will define competitive health AI over the next five years. B is a defensible fallback if regulatory pressure intensifies.

Option D threads the needle: inference and personalization happen in the cloud (full capability, normal cost), training on user data requires affirmative opt-in with clear UX. Users who want to contribute to better coaching can; users who don't, don't. The default-off training stance is the honest one for health data and is what a regulator, journalist, or plaintiff's lawyer will judge favorably.

**Implementation conditions that make D work:**
1. Inference infrastructure should still use encryption in transit and at rest, with access controls auditable to enterprise standards.
2. Opt-in UX must be genuinely neutral, not dark-patterned — this is the trust premise.
3. Publish a transparency report on opt-in rates and training data composition.
4. Commit publicly to never reclassifying opt-in as opt-out.

D buys capability and trust. That combination wins in health.
